speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five. November 26, 1996, interview with Sonia Bielski. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You were telling us about the various workshops and the jobs of the people. Was there a hierarchy? Was it more important to do one thing than to do another?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was important. The young, they went for the food, and to fight. Because even for the food, when you went, you have to be always fighting on the roads. Because there was not only the Bielskis, but there were other partisans. There were Polish partisans, and Russian partisans. You have to be very careful for all of them. Because they hate us there. They hate the Jew. This is it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they had afraid of us. The same thing. They're very afraid of us. Now, who was in the [NON-ENGLISH]? Who was working in the colony, yeah? Let's put this in the colony. This was very good organized. This we have to give a lot of credit to Tuvia's people. They were organized. They were baking bread. They were baking dinners. They were-- They had a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what's a [NON-ENGLISH]? A [NON-ENGLISH] is a woman [NON-ENGLISH]. A bath?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bath, yes. They had a [NON-ENGLISH]. Yeah. They should be clean. They had a place where somebody was very sick with typhus. That's far away from the camp. There were nurses there, and a doctor. And they take care on this sick people. They were making, fixing the shoes. They were making shoes for the-- for the higher people. This was something, in the time from the war, to have something like that, but not the young. This was all the older people. The young were busy always fighting. And fighting not only to partisan, fighting for the food. Because 1,000 people have to eat. One has to eat. But 1,000 people, we have to feed them. And there was no hunger. If somebody would tell you that it was hunger, you cannot say. If you have bread and water and potatoes, you cannot be hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are any people that couldn't contribute any work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe, yeah. I remember one thing. I was nothing to [NON-ENGLISH]. I came only to visit my parents and to visit Chaya, Asael, and Tuvia. We never came in. So they took us, like you know what. But I saw whatever I saw. This was beautiful organized. You cannot say nothing against them. Beautiful organized. I cannot say nothing against that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the organization done? How many people were in the administration?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was probably about eight, 10 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Lazar Malbin, Raja Kopinska, my friend Lewin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Malbin Lazar-- [INAUDIBLE] Hirsele Nochinowski. Another couple of this, but I don't know them, because remember one thing-- I am from Novogrudok. But I didn't know them. I know the people only what I went to school with them. And that's what I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then that's maybe other ones who'll tell you that they know everybody. OK. I tell the truth. I didn't know them. And to this day, when I'm coming to Israel, everybody is coming to me. Sonia, how are you? I said, OK. And in the beginning, I said to my friends, Raja, who are they? She said, ask how is the family? Don't ask about nobody else. How I ask about the family? Because you will begin to see who, who. They will tell something is wrong with you. You don't remember them. So that's what I'm saying. How are you family? They said, OK. You have to tell that you know them. But that's not true. You don't have to tell them that you don't know them. Because they think that you are over them, you understand? This is something what they didn't like it. You know? I want to go back for a moment, regarding getting food. How did you keep the population quiet about the raids to get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody knew where to go to ask for the food. All the zimlanka knew that these days, they're coming to take the food. The other days, somebody else is coming to take the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What I mean is, how did you to keep the peasants quiet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the peasants? They had afraid of us. They knew that if something, something will happen, the smallest thing, they will kill the families, or shoot them. They know that the Bielskis kill the families with the children, with the whole of everything. If something, somebody came and said that he said something to the German, they were killing the whole village. They had afraid of us. Only that they had afraid. They didn't do nothing. Whoever did, he never saw the day. He never saw the day of tomorrow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They was killing them. Remember one thing, that Zusia, Alex, my husband, before they began only, the partisans, he was never in a ghetto. He was never in the house. The young Polish girls, they were covering him, but them in the house. And they were feeding them. And they have brothers in the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the brothers knew after that, that she was holding the Bielskis. They killed the sister. Do you understand what I told you now? He could live the whole three years. But not one, but three women, young, rich, they built for him a place to sit. Allow me to go back for a moment, regarding the internal life of the camp. How were deaths handled? Were there rituals? Did people bury people ritually, if somebody died?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. And I don't know. I will tell you the truth. I know that we lost about 300 people from the time of the war. They buried them. I don't know the this and that. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other Jewish rituals observed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yeah, there were older that they were praying. My mother was praying always, by her in the place. By her in the zimlanka. Nothing else. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any births, pregnancies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was pregnancy. But they try to do something with that, not to have the children. So we had a doctor. Sometimes the doctor did it there. Or sometimes we went to [? Zorin. ?] That was a-- [? Zorin's ?] place, a partisan too. A group of partisan. So they went there to make this. But they didn't-- I don't think somebody had a child in the partisan, no. No. They try to get rid of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] living with one of they high administrators. How did they assign jobs to people? By what method were people told what they had to do, or when they had to go on a raid?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was decided in the evening, that this and this group, 10 or 15 people, they going in this direction. And they have to bring this and this from the food. This group will have to go here and here. They didn't go, stop, I'm going. No. This was decided. And the people had to go. They didn't go all of them in one place. They went in a couple of places. Because they would kill them if they would go all together. So they went-- this was decided. And it was decided that one of them will take the-- in his hand. And he will be-- he will-- he will watch them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They should not rob. Because there were places where they were robbing. And then they were telling this to Platon. And he was called the Bielskis. He said, what kind of partisan? You robbing? And this was [? Zorin's ?] bit, so they stopped this too. And they were [INAUDIBLE] in the partisans were many Jew what they were [NON-ENGLISH]. And they were saying that the Bielskis are against the [? constable. ?] Yeah. Meaning against communism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Against communism. And they wanted get ready of the brother Bielskis. Platon by themselves, he was telling us this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Bielskis control what the people, who went to do the raids, did?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were telling us. They were telling us. Whatever they did, not warn, they killed a couple of them. And this was quiet for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who killed a couple of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Bielskis. It was people what they had long this. And they were telling about us that we are against Stalin. And they tried to destroy the partisans, our partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they control the inside people who did not listen to them, and did rob?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we put them in the jail. We had a small jail. You put them in the jail for a couple days, for a week. And then let them out. And they learned their lesson. We had for everything. We had a medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the people in the administration in agreement about what to do all the time about whom to allow to come in, or about other policies? Did they agree with each other all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three brothers had to agree. This was the end of that. Only three brothers had to agree. The other one went under them. You understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any serious disagreement about anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes it was. But you see, they were always trying to cover this, the brothers. They didn't want this should be too much, talking about it. So they were covered. There were a little-- they would quarrel about this. But in the end, they were agreeing of everything together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you aware of any disagreement about major things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I agree-- I was. And I knew. Because my husband told me. My husband told me that was a big agreement-- Tuvia wanted too many people to bring to the forest. People who didn't know how to shoot-- women, children, and old ones. And Zusia said, if you will bring so many people, who all will go done. Because this is impossible. You have whatever you have. We have to already take care on that. But you cannot take no more. That's impossible. Because they will take us under. And this was a big argument. But this I have to give Tuvia credit. He wanted-- I don't care if you kill another German, another German. I want to take it out of this one. The old one, the lady, whatever. This was Tuvia. But Zusia and Asael, they didn't agree with him. But the end, they let go a little of that. They let go to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, um--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was other side-- other things too. The Moscow ask lot of money for the ammunition what they sending. So we said, that we don't have a money-- we don't have money, because the German took away everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they said, no, you have something. You have to find. So then, it was a session. And they asked everybody if he has a watch. Who has something they should give the-- because we needed. We needed for Moscow. So not everybody was very happy with that. But they took. They send a little. And I don't know what happened, the other ones. And I don't care about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was many things like that. We bought for the gold, we bought salt. Because very bad was the salt in the time of the war. So we bought medicine for the gold. We tried everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any opposition to the-- Tuvia's leadership?

speaker: interviewee
text: Opposition? In the beginning. That's not against Tuvia. But against the brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was about 10 people. In the beginning this was probably the beginning of, by the end of '42. By the end of '42. Yeah. 10 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they decided they don't want it no more, the Bielskis. This with little, for not too many people then. This probably about 20, 30 people. They don't want no more the Bielskis. Why the Bielskis telling them to do that and do that? So they said, the three brothers, they begin to talk about it. And they said, OK. You want to go? Go ahead. Leave everything, the ammunition, and go. So they left the ammunition, and they went.

speaker: interviewee
text: After two, three, four days, they came crying and begging to come back. And they took them back. And they were already OK then. But this was something like that. I don't want to talk about it. Because other people live still. And they have children. And I don't want to say something what I will be sorry for that. Because the wives are telling us it never was like that. But this was like that. And I ask a couple of people what they were with them. And they said, yes, you're right. This was like that. And that's it. They let go about the war. They wanted to go away. They wanted to make by themselves [INAUDIBLE] a partisans. Not the Bielskis. Without the Bielskis.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they left, where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The went from one forest to the other forest. But they didn't know where to go and what to do. So they were, they run-- till the food run out, so they came begging. They want to come back. And they took them back. And they were very good partisans. And at the end, they were very good partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that at some point, the [INAUDIBLE] was moved into the puszta. How does one move 1,200 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was divided in groups. Groups from 10 to 20 people, and everybody, one commander had 20 people, or 25 people. And he went today. After three, four days, another group went. And group after group, they went like that. And we didn't lose no one. And everybody came to the puszta. But this was OK. But coming out from the puszta, this is something else. One commander-- I went with Zusia. I had a horse. A small horse. Zusia had a German horse, a black one. Something, something. After the war he had her all the time. And I had a small horse. And she went with him, like she was the same thing. A beautiful horse. And we came in. We were about 20 people, riders. And we saw came nearer, we saw a woman with a child sitting under the tree, and crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zusia went down from the horse, and said, why you crying? What happened? She said, this and this, throw me out. He said, I don't have a wife and I don't need you. Not you and your [INAUDIBLE]. My wife and my children are dead. And I don't want to take care on you. Who said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Said the commander of this group, to this woman. He said, who was the commander? She said, this, and this, and this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give us the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my husband said to everybody, to the group with the horses, and me, you stay here, and I will send somebody for the commander. The commander was from your [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Sure. That's our groups. And he send. And he came. He took them in. And he said, how come we gave it to you 20 people, you left a woman with a child and, like that, without food, without nothing? Who gave it to you permission to say so? You are eating our bread, and you go in with us. He said, listen to me. You have a wife. Take care of your wife. I don't have a wife. I don't need to take care on her.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no. So he took out this, and he killed him, on the place. He was sick a week, Zusia, after that. But the lesson was a very good lesson. The lesson was very good. Because he was only the first one, but other one too. He said, my wife and the children are already long dead. What for I have to take care on them?

speaker: interviewee
text: But this lesson what he gave to him, would never find another woman or child in the forest by themselves. OK. When we came to Israel, the wife from this man, said, you know, you are a killer. [NON-ENGLISH] was the whole-- something. This is for the Bielskis. They did something [NON-ENGLISH]. And they give them the orders, and everything. They said, you're a murderer.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, Zusia, who are you? She said, you killed my husband. He said, why did I kill your husband? Because he left a woman and a child, not his, in the forest. Why did you not take her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, and he said, I'm very sorry that I killed him. I'm very sorry. But he was worth it to be killed. And this was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was his wife and the child when he was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but while--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. At the time that he had left behind the other woman and the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was his wife?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was with another man. She was with another man in another group. Later he took this wife from another man. And he make her his wife. They changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how was discipline enforced with people who would not listen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They listened. There was enforced. There was enforced. Because if this was not enforced, nobody would stay alive. They knew that they have to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the forest? How long was the duration of the whole stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three and a half years. Three and a half years. From '42 till we went out from the forest in '44, after the German. The German left our place in Novogrudok. And we went out with them together, in '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that about 300 people died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What they die of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they died in the-- in the fights. We were fighting them behind the German. How do I have this everything for the German? How to kill somebody and everything. We took away from the German, who was killing them. We were hiding in the forest, under the tree, we seen 10, 15 people German was riding, or going by car, yes. And we were fighting them, and stop the German. And we killed them and took the whole-- the whole [NON-ENGLISH]. The whole--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ammunition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition with us. And that's what we got the ammunition. Later we bought the ammunition from Moscow. We had more. We had enough of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that people did not know how to fight. Who ultimately taught them how to shoot, how to fight?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We have people, what they never were in the army. Jewish people didn't go to army, and the Polish too. They were trying to go out from the army. They were trying to hit a finger. I am without a finger. I didn't go to the army. I am sick. From the doctor something, they didn't go to the army. So when they came to the partisan, we have to help them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we give it to them this ammunition. And we had a couple of people, what they were in the Polish army. And they were showing them how to fight. If he didn't know, he was-- they were calling him, how do you say? [NON-ENGLISH] whatever. No, [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know. Many things they calling him. And he-- they left him. But if was-- he know already how to shoot, he went with the other boys for food. Or he was watching the horses, watching the wagons, see? When you say they left him, they left him where?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left him on the wagon. He was watching the food. He did something. He had to do something. They didn't feed him without nothing. He have to give something for himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were liberated in the end of, or in the middle of '44, in '44, when the German left the [NON-ENGLISH], left the west from Poland. And they were running away. The German was running away. When they were running away, we were killing them in the forest. They came to our zimlankas. They killed then by us, about four people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They killed partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah-ha. Yeah. They came. And they saw partisans there. Because we went for something, I don't know what happened. And they killed four partisan, ours. But we killed them many, many. We didn't-- we didn't gave it to them a moment to rest. We killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the women know how to shoot too?

speaker: interviewee
text: There maybe were a couple. Not me. I had always a big seven piece revolver. But I am-- I was afraid always they should not shot-- I should not be shot from my revolver by myself. This was for self defense. My husband gave it to me. If something happened, I should have how to kill myself than to go into the German, you understand? But I never shot. I never stay on the-- at night to watch the German, nothing. No, I cannot say it. But there were people, maybe ladies, what they weren't for night watching them, yes. I don't think it was shooting.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I know my sister, Chaya, yes? Asael's wife. She said that she went. And she was shooting. And she was doing everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: In head. In the head. She was thinking that she was doing that. But OK. She wants like that. It's OK. I cannot say that I did something what I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Polish partisan groups in the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The Polish, they were killing the Jewish girls. They find somebody. In a second, they were killing them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they just find the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Sometimes a girl went on to look for a [NON-ENGLISH], for something, something. For [NON-ENGLISH]. What is it called for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mushrooms and berries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mushrooms. It was something. They were killing them. This is the first time and the last time, never went out by themselves. And this was only once. And this was their lessons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any non-Jewish partisans together with you in your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was Jewish. The Bielskis. The Bielskis was 1,000 people, only Jewish. There were many Jew in the Russian, in the Russian partisan. But like a Jewish partisan like the Bielskis, that's was only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish partisans and the Russian [NON-ENGLISH] disclose that there were Jews? Did they know there were Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They were coming to us. They coming to eat, and to talk, and to flirt with the girls. Yeah. They were. They were [NON-ENGLISH]. There were another partisan, the Russian. But they're Jew, what they were. But the Russian, they were the perfection, the perfection. They did everything what no Russian could do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] You know what is it? They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: To explode trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trains. They did everything. Everything. They were exceptional, wonderful. They were coming to us, sure. They were coming. They feel by us, like home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they originally with the Russian army? Who were they? The Jewish guys?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were people--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Russian partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were people from our cities surrounding there, from Lida, from Novogrudok, from Baranowizce.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they wound up in the Russian partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wound up, because before the Bielskis organize, they went in there. They went into the Russian. You understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to break now.
